data.title: Provident Financial Holdings Should Trade At Single-Digit Multiples
data.description:
Provident Financial Holdings (NASDAQ:PROV) is a community bank in Riverside County, California.

The bank has not grown in long time, and is exposed mostly to residential mortgages (about 50/50 single and multi-family) in SoCal. The bank was able to weather the post-COVID interest rate volatility by being more exposed to variable-rate mortgages, but this also reduces the potential improvement from lower rates forward.

In general, I believe a stagnant bank with low exposure to positive tailwinds is not attractive at around 16x earnings. In addition, we should mention the higher near-term risk from SoCal economic exposure to deportations and to trade restrictions. I initiate with a Hold

Suburb SoCal bank

Provident Financial Holdings (not to be confused with Provident Financial Services (PFS) or Provident Bancorp (PVBC)) is a 13-branch community bank with 12 of its offices located in Riverside County and one in San Bernardino County.

Riverside County is a large urban, suburban, and even rural county that includes the western part of the Greater Los Angeles metro (the area known as the Inland Empire), plus smaller towns in the desert mountain area from LA to the border with Arizona.

Lending outside of those areas

According to the company's investor presentation (page 13), only 20% of the company's loan assets are within its operational area, in the counties of Riverside and San Bernardino.

A remaining 44% is within Southern California (no mention of specific counties, neither on the presentation nor the 10-K), and another 36% in 'Other California', meaning the rest of California (again with no specifics).

The fact that the company is lending outside of its area of operations, and that it does not disclose exactly where it is lending, adds a little uncertainty to its loan book.

Context-specific risks

Two of the current administration's policies affect the SoCal (and therefore PROV's loan book) very directly.

First, deportations. Independent of the view about the correctness of the deportations, they will impact the region more than almost anywhere else in the country. According to some research, almost 20% of all undocumented immigrants in the US live in California. When people are deported, it brings many (at least short-term) negative effects: there is less available labor, increasing labor costs for companies and people; there is less demand for housing and services; there is more fear among families, reducing their willingness to spend or invest; and some families might lose part of their income-earning members.

Second, tariffs. Los Angeles is the busiest port in the US, by a wide margin. This has created a large industry in neighboring counties and towns. Indeed, Provident discloses on its latest 10-K that logistics is the number one employer industry in San Bernardino and number three in Riverside. Again, lower trade impacts logistics, which impacts employment, and potentially credit risk.

Mostly residential mortgages

The company's assets are almost entirely exposed to residential mortgages. Loans are 90% of the company's balance sheet (almost all securities outside of loans are MBS), and 92% of loans are residential mortgages.

However, not all residential mortgages are equal. The company lends about 55/45% to single-family and multi-family borrowers. The economics and risks of these loans are very different. A single-family loan is given to a person or family, and is usually paid back from labor income. A multi-family loan is given to a company to finance the purchase of several units, which are then resold or rented, and from these proceeds are interest and amortization repaid. Therefore, multi-family has higher execution risks.

Because the majority of these loans are amortizing and were originated before 2022 (only 23% of single-family and 14% of multi-family since 2023), the average LTVs are low (63% for single-family, 43% for multi-family), implying that, on average, the capital at risk is low.

Low(er) exposure to rate fluctuations

90% of the company's loan assets are variable or adjustable-rate loans. As described in the company's 10-K, the majority of its residential loans (single or multi-family) use SOFR as the benchmark, plus a margin.

The adjustment takes some time (there is a limit to periodical increases and to the lifetime increase of the rate), but this is a much faster adjustment mechanism to rates on the asset side than for fixed-rate loans.

Additionally, in fixed-rate loans, loan duration increases with higher rates, because people prefer to 'lock in' previous lower rates. This does not happen with adjusting rates as much.

The result was that the company's NIM has not moved that much compared to other banks, after the FED started its current tightening cycle in 2022.

On the securities book, the company is more exposed to lower rates, because the majority of its MBS securities have yields below 2%, and are therefore underwater in their principal.

Variable rates increase credit risk

There is one scenario where variable rates become a very negative liability, and that is if rates become very high. Although variable-rate loans have clauses that limit their potential lifetime increase, if rates go up a lot, they increase the burden on the debtor, whereas fixed-rate mortgages actually become cheaper (in relative terms). This should be taken into account in an scenario where expectations are for higher, not lower rates, like we have today.

Positive but eroding cost advantage

Like many small community and regional banks, the company has a base of low-cost deposits. As of FY25, the aggregate cost was 1.34% for deposits. At least 60% of deposits pay little to no interest expenses.

However, probably because of the low yield, non-interest-bearing or low-interest-bearing deposits are decreasing over time as a percentage of total deposits. For example, from FY24 to FY25, total deposits went down $40 million, while time deposits, which pay interest, went up $40 million. We can also observe below how deposits cover an increasingly lower portion of loans. The rest is covered by interbank financing, which pays rates closer to SOFR.

Bad efficiency from small scale

Probably one of the most important factors in bank operations is the efficiency ratio (the ratio of non-interest OpEx to net interest income). While the FDIC shows that the average bank has an efficiency ratio of 56%, Provident's figures are closer to 75/80%.

This indicates either a lower level of cost-consciousness or problems with scale, or both. The bank might need to grow over time to cover increasing labor and lease costs. In this respect, the bank has not grown its assets in several years already.

Valuation is not attractive

On a TTM basis, the company earned $6.3 million in net income. As commented, compared to other banks, which have a more positive exposure to decreasing rates, PROV is a little bit more shielded from that (and from the problems that come with higher rates too).

That is, we cannot expect a meaningful improvement in earnings coming from lower rates. We also cannot expect much growth, as the bank has barely grown in more than a decade. PROV also has significant exposure to the worst trends in the US economy (deportations and tariffs), and has a high (i.e., bad) efficiency ratio.

In general, this is a bank that I believe should trade at single-digit P/Es at most. However, its market cap is $102 million, or 16x earnings.

I do not see a scenario where it increases earnings or why it should trade at even higher multiples; therefore, there's little upside potential. Contrary to this, if the economy weakens, both earnings and the multiple might contract, exposing the shareholder to losses.

For these reasons, I believe PROV is not an opportunity and initiate with a Hold.
